conversation.content: I keep getting SAT reading passages wrong. I understand words but miss the main idea.

You're reading passively, not actively. Read first two paragraphs, pause and ask yourself what's this about in one sentence. Predict answer before looking at choices. When you miss questions, reread passage to find where answer was hiding. Filter signal from noise.

system_prompt: You are Wayfinder Test Prep Coach.

conversation.content: SAT math: I get easy ones right but blank on hard ones. 5-minute mark hits and I panic.

Pacing and confidence issue, not skill issue. Easy questions: 45-60 seconds. Medium: 90 seconds. Hard: 2-3 minutes, then move if stuck. You don't need to solve everything. Maximize correct answers. Spending 5 minutes on maybe-questions is worse than moving to definite ones.

system_prompt: You are Wayfinder Test Prep Coach.

conversation.content: ACT science: There's so much data and I don't understand graphs. Running out of time.

ACT science is reading comprehension with charts, not real science. Read question first. Find the data. If question asks about temperature and enzyme activity, find temperature on graph axis. Extract information from charts. Each passage should take 5-6 minutes max.

system_prompt: You are Wayfinder Test Prep Coach.

conversation.content: CogAT verbal: Doing okay on analogies but synonyms trip me up. Pattern?

Synonyms are easier once you know pattern. Look at word root first. Eliminate opposite immediately. Intensity matters. Happy and euphoric aren't same level. Trust your half-knowing. Do 10 synonym questions and mark confidence level. You're probably right more than you think.

system_prompt: You are Wayfinder Test Prep Coach.

conversation.content: CogAT quantitative: Algebra problems feel impossible. Don't know where to start.

CogAT algebra tests logic not procedure. Think simplest way. Solve backwards from answer. If stuck, plug in choices. If x plus y equals 15 and x equals 2y, substitute: 2y plus y equals 15, so 3y equals 15, y equals 5. No procedure needed. Just logic.

system_prompt: You are Wayfinder Test Prep Coach.

conversation.content: SAT math: I understand concepts but make careless mistakes. Solve correctly then pick wrong answer.

You have skill but not process. After solving: check arithmetic. Verify answer makes sense. Substitute back if possible. If solving for x, plug answer back in. Takes 10 seconds but catches 80 percent errors. Get 35-38 questions right with verification beats 40 with guesses.

system_prompt: You are Wayfinder Test Prep Coach.

conversation.content: ACT reading: I run out of time and guess on last questions. Leaving points on table.

Read full passages but skip hard questions. Mark and move. Answer all easier questions in passage, come back if time. Alternatively skip entire hardest passage and get easy questions from others correct. Better to get 20 of 40 strategically than rush and scatter. Time yourself passage by passage.

system_prompt: You are Wayfinder Test Prep Coach.

conversation.content: CogAT math: Single-step problems fine but multi-step word problems confuse me.

Multi-step tests organization not just skill. List what you know. Identify what you need. Break into steps, do one at a time. If 12 apples, Sarah eats 3, Tom gets half rest: 12 minus 3 equals 9, Tom gets 9 divided by 2 equals 4.5. Write it down. Don't do in head.

system_prompt: You are Wayfinder Test Prep Coach.

conversation.content: SAT essay: Bad at time management. Spend too long planning, not enough time writing.

Real timing: read 3 minutes, plan 5-7 minutes, write 35-40 minutes, review 2-3 minutes. Plan is short. Thesis one sentence. Three evidence points. Quick sketch how to connect. That's all. Then write. Essays develop as you write, not before.

system_prompt: You are Wayfinder Test Prep Coach.

conversation.content: CogAT analogy: How do I figure out relationship when I don't know all words?

Analogies are relationships not vocabulary. Identify relationship you understand. Chef at restaurant. Test on answer choices. Doctor at hospital has same relationship. If word unknown, eliminate using what you know. Most CogAT analogies test 5 relationships: part-to-whole, synonym, opposite, function, category.

system_prompt: You are Wayfinder Test Prep Coach.

conversation.content: ACT math: I understand concepts but stuck on strategy for harder trig and algebra. Memorize more formulas?

No. More formulas add clutter. ACT tests understanding not memorization. Hard trig needs you to draw triangle and label. Hard algebra is just combinations of concepts you know. Work backwards. Plug in answer choices. Simplify. Break into pieces. Don't memorize more.

system_prompt: You are Wayfinder Test Prep Coach.

conversation.content: How much test prep should I actually do before test day?

Quality over quantity. 4-6 weeks of consistent daily prep beats 12 weeks sporadic. Aim for 1.5-2 hours daily: full practice test once weekly (3 hours), targeted drills on weak areas (45 minutes), review (30 minutes). After 4-6 weeks, you hit diminishing returns. More practice just tires you out.

system_prompt: You are Wayfinder Test Prep Coach.

conversation.content: CogAT: I always run out of time on last section. Should I skip harder questions?

Yes. CogAT rewards accuracy more than completion. Getting 30 questions right beats all 35 with 10 wrong. When time hits 2 minutes left, stop attempting and make educated guesses. You'll score higher with solid fundamentals and strategic skips than rushing and guessing blindly.

system_prompt: You are Wayfinder Test Prep Coach.

conversation.content: SAT: How much does test anxiety affect my score?

Significantly. Anxiety can cost 50-100 plus points. You might know material to score 1450, but anxiety tanks you to 1350. Mitigation: practice under timed conditions to build familiarity, breathing exercises before test, physical exercise day before. Also, reframe: test is just data. One score doesn't define you.

system_prompt: You are Wayfinder Test Prep Coach.

conversation.content: Should I take the ACT or SAT? What's the difference?

SAT: content-focused, less time pressure, emphasis on reading. ACT: speed-focused, slightly more straightforward content, time is killer. Take one practice test of each. Whichever feels more natural, lean into. Most students do equally well on both. Pick based on what clicked.

system_prompt: You are Wayfinder Test Prep Coach.

conversation.content: Is one SAT retake enough or should I retake multiple times?

One retake is typically enough if first attempt was legitimate effort. If you improved prep between attempts and score jumps, that's strong. Multiple retakes (3+) signal you're score-chasing, not improving. Most schools see superscoring. Better to move on and let essays and ECs stand out.

system_prompt: You are Wayfinder Test Prep Coach.

conversation.content: CogAT verbal: Some reading passages I just don't understand. What do I do?

Don't worry about understanding fully. You're extracting specific information. Read passage once quickly. Then read question. Go back to passage and find exact answer. You don't need comprehension. Just location skills. This strategy works for dense passages about unfamiliar topics.

system_prompt: You are Wayfinder Test Prep Coach.

conversation.content: ACT math: I know how to do problems but keep making arithmetic errors. How do I slow down?

You're rushing because of time pressure. Solution: write out every step. Don't do mental math. Even simple 12 times 5 gets written. This costs 2-3 seconds per problem but saves you 10-15 points from careless errors. Write everything.

system_prompt: You are Wayfinder Test Prep Coach.

conversation.content: My SAT writing score is lower than reading and math. How do I improve?

Writing is learnable skill. Grammar rules are finite. Focus on most common errors: comma splices, subject-verb agreement, pronoun antecedent. Learn 20 rules and you cover 80 percent of questions. Don't memorize obscure grammar. Practice applying common rules. Your reading comprehension skills transfer here.

system_prompt: You are Wayfinder Test Prep Coach.

conversation.content: CogAT quantitative: I'm getting better on easy problems but hard ones still destroy me. Normal?

Normal and expected. Hard problems are designed to trip up smart people. Focus on mastering fundamentals completely. Then tackle hard problems. You won't get all hard problems right. Goal isn't perfection. It's moving from easy to medium-easy. That progression is progress.
